Lee](https://s3.amazonaws.com/NumberAnal/image/Sarah_Lee_120.jpg) Sarah Lee AI generated Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8 5 min read· May 28, 2025 12 views [![Image 4: Matrix movie still](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1526374965328-7f61d4dc18c5?crop=entropy&cs=tinysrgb&fit=max&fm=jpg&ixid=M3w3MDgyMDR8MHwxfHNlYXJjaHwxfHxkaWdpdGFsJTIwb2Jzb2xlc2NlbmNlJTJDJTIwZGlnaXRhbCUyMGxpdGVyYXR1cmUlMkMlMjBjdWx0dXJhbCUyMGhlcml0YWdlJTJDJTIwZGlnaXRhbCUyMHByZXNlcnZhdGlvbnxlbnwwfDB8fHwxNzQ4NDUxMjgyfDA&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&q=80&w=500&?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1526374965328-7f61d4dc18c5?crop=entropy&cs=tinysrgb&fit=max&fm=jpg&ixid=M3w3MDgyMDR8MHwxfHNlYXJjaHwxfHxkaWdpdGFsJTIwb2Jzb2xlc2NlbmNlJTJDJTIwZGlnaXRhbCUyMGxpdGVyYXR1cmUlMkMlMjBjdWx0dXJhbCUyMGhlcml0YWdlJTJDJTIwZGlnaXRhbCUyMHByZXNlcnZhdGlvbnxlbnwwfDB8fHwxNzQ4NDUxMjgyfDA&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&q=80&w=500&?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral) Photo by [Markus Spiske](https://unsplash.com/@markusspiske?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral) The rapid advancement of technology has led to an unprecedented era of digital content creation, dissemination, and consumption. However, this has also resulted in a growing concern about the long-term preservation of digital information. The phenomenon of digital obsolescence has become a pressing issue, threatening the very fabric of our cultural and literary heritage. In this article, we will explore the causes and effects of digital obsolescence on literature and cultural heritage, and discuss strategies for preserving digital content for future generations. Causes of Digital Obsolescence ------------------------------ Digital obsolescence refers to the situation where digital content becomes inaccessible or unreadable due to changes in technology, formats, or user behaviors. There are several factors contributing to digital obsolescence, including: ### Rapid Technological Advancements and Changing User Behaviors The pace of technological innovation is accelerating, leading to a constant stream of new devices, software, and platforms. As a result, older technologies and formats become obsolete, making it challenging to access and preserve digital content created using outdated technologies. For instance, the shift from physical media like CDs and DVDs to streaming services has rendered many older formats obsolete. ### Format and Technology Obsolescence The rapid evolution of digital formats and technologies has led to a situation where content created in older formats becomes inaccessible. For example, the once-popular 3.5-inch floppy disks and Zip drives are now relics of the past, and the data stored on them is largely inaccessible without specialized hardware and software. ### Lack of Standardization and Compatibility The lack of standardization across different platforms, devices, and software applications has resulted in compatibility issues, making it difficult to access and preserve digital content. For instance, proprietary file formats used by certain software applications may become obsolete when the software is no longer supported or updated. The following diagram illustrates the factors contributing to digital obsolescence: Impact on Digital Literature ---------------------------- The effects of digital obsolescence on literature and cultural heritage are far-reaching and multifaceted. Some of the key impacts include: ### Loss of Cultural and Literary Heritage Digital obsolescence can result in the loss of cultural and literary heritage, as digital content becomes inaccessible or is deleted. This can have significant consequences for researchers, scholars, and the general public, who rely on digital content to understand and appreciate our cultural and literary past. ### Inaccessibility of Digital Content As digital formats and technologies become obsolete, the content created using them becomes inaccessible. This can lead to a situation where valuable digital content is lost forever, unless efforts are made to preserve it. ### Difficulty in Preserving Digital Artifacts Digital artifacts, such as e-books, digital art, and online collections, are vulnerable to digital obsolescence. Preserving these artifacts requires significant technical expertise, resources, and infrastructure. The following table highlights some examples of digital content that are vulnerable to digital obsolescence: | Type of Digital Content | Examples | | --- | --- | | E-books and digital publications | Online literary journals, e-books, digital archives | | Digital art and media | Digital art installations, online exhibitions, multimedia content | | Online collections and archives | Digital museum collections, online archives, historical records | Strategies for Preservation --------------------------- To mitigate the effects of digital obsolescence, several strategies can be employed to preserve digital content for future generations. Some of these strategies include: ### Emulation and Migration Strategies Emulation involves creating an environment that mimics the original technology or software used to create the digital content, allowing it to be accessed and used on newer systems. Migration involves transferring digital content from an older format or technology to a newer one, ensuring that it remains accessible. For example, the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine uses emulation to preserve older websites and web pages, allowing users to access them as they were originally published. ### Digital Repository and Archival Practices Digital repositories and archives play a crucial role in preserving digital content. These institutions use various strategies, such as digital curation, metadata management, and preservation metadata, to ensure that digital content remains accessible and usable over time. The following diagram illustrates the process of digital curation: ### Collaboration and Community Engagement Collaboration and community engagement are essential for preserving digital content. By working together, institutions, researchers, and the general public can share knowledge, resources, and expertise to ensure that digital content is preserved for future generations. For instance, the Digital Preservation Coalition brings together institutions and individuals from around the world to share best practices, research, and expertise in digital preservation. Conclusion ---------- Digital obsolescence is a pressing issue that threatens the long-term preservation of our cultural and literary heritage. By understanding the causes and effects of digital obsolescence, we can develop effective strategies for preserving digital content. Emulation and migration strategies, digital repository and archival practices, and collaboration and community engagement are essential for ensuring that digital content remains accessible and usable over time. As we move forward in this digital age, it is crucial that we prioritize the preservation of digital content. By doing so, we can ensure that our cultural and literary heritage is protected for future generations. References ---------- 1. [The Digital Preservation Coalition](https://www.dpconline.org/) 2. [The Internet Archive's Wayback Machine](https://web.archive.org/) 3. [Digital Obsolescence: A Threat to Cultural Heritage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780128124803000135) 4. [Preserving Digital Content](https://www.clir.org/pubs/reports/pub121/) 5. [Digital Curation: A Guide to Creating and Managing Digital Collections](https://www.alastore.ala.org/content/digital-curation-guide-creating-and-managing-digital-collections) FAQ --- ### What is digital obsolescence? Digital obsolescence refers to the situation where digital content becomes inaccessible or unreadable due to changes in technology, formats, or user behaviors. ### Why is digital obsolescence a problem? Digital obsolescence is a problem because it can result in the loss of cultural and literary heritage, as digital content becomes inaccessible or is deleted. ### How can we prevent digital obsolescence? To prevent digital obsolescence, we can employ strategies such as emulation and migration, digital repository and archival practices, and collaboration and community engagement. ### What is emulation? Emulation involves creating an environment that mimics the original technology or software used to create the digital content, allowing it to be accessed and used on newer systems. ### What is digital curation? Digital curation involves the selection, preservation, and maintenance of digital content over time, ensuring that it remains accessible and usable. ![Image 5: Sarah Lee](https://s3.amazonaws.com/NumberAnal/image/Sarah_Lee_120.jpg)Sarah Lee 2025-05-28 09:54:42 * * * #### 0 Comments * * * You need to be logged in to add comments. Start your discovery ![](https://artic-web.imgix.net/d335c986-7075-4753-a84f-9cb11876ac77/navigation-thumbnail-events.jpg?auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=auto&blur=1200&sat=20) Join us for a wide range of programs—there's something for visitors of all ages. Check out the calendar ![]() Learn more ## Secondary Navigation ![Nam June Paik, Family of Robot: Baby, 1986](https://artic-web.imgix.net/null05465f52-e850-4006-a882-9de143bca8e7/Baby_Robot.jpg?rect=749%2C1392%2C1958%2C1093&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=419&blur=1200&sat=20) ![Nam June Paik, Family of Robot: Baby, 1986](https://artic-web.imgix.net/null05465f52-e850-4006-a882-9de143bca8e7/Baby_Robot.jpg?rect=749%2C1392%2C1958%2C1093&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=419&blur=1200&sat=20) # Saving Digital Art: Video Migrations, Obsolescence, and Other Tales In the Lab ## Share Kristin M. MacDonough October 3, 2019 Do you remember the first text document you saved or the first video you saw on YouTube? By Kristin M. MacDonough Most of us don’t. Much has been written about how thoroughly the digital realm has become integrated into our daily lives. We take photos and videos with our smartphones, documenting everything from the mundane to the social and political events shaping our world, and we can share these widely with friends and strangers. We can engage with video content across platforms like YouTube and Vimeo and participate using Skype or Google Hangouts. I’m writing this blog post using Google Docs because I will be able to access and edit this text on any device: desktop computer, laptop, smartphone. My colleague editing this can provide feedback using suggestions and comments on their own devices … in real time. ![A work made of two color videos, sound, two monitors on two pedestals; tape i (male): 60 min. loop; tape ii (female): 52 min. loop.](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhBgAFAPQAABkcDTc9QFVZWFthX2prZ2hqa3Nvam10dpmampyfn5efoqKioqKkpKOkpqSlpaKorqqsra6wss/Oz9PS09ra2tzc3N/f3t7e39/e4OHg4ODh4+np6vj3+Pv4+wAAAAAAACH5BAAAAAAALAAAAAAGAAUAAAUYIKVV1pYhxuIQjAAMRXBAStI80dVNEoeFADs=) The digital is in our artwork as well. Today, digital art materials enter the museum in two ways: as digitized content, reformatted from an analog source such as 16mm film or video tape, or as born-digital art, created using tools like digital cameras or software applications. Sometimes, part of the artwork *is* a software application. So how do museums make sure digital artworks are not trapped in time and obsolete technologies? ![A work made of single-channel video sculpture: thirteen television monitors and aluminum armature; color, silent. number nine of nine unique sculptures in series..](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAwAFAPMAAJR9aZWBcpuGdIiIiJmYmrKztr/Dx8DDx9DX3tLY3tTZ39ve4uTq8erw+AAAAAAAACH5BAAAAAAALAAAAAADAAUAAAQLkDW2ikIjGXICEBEAOw==) **Inherent Fragility** When we think about art conservation, [examining paintings](/articles/730/scratching-the-surface-a-close-look-at-cross-sections), [reassembling a sculpture](/articles/695/piecing-together-a-renaissance-masterpiece), or [photograph treatment](/articles/696/encounter-with-unusual-photo-paper) might come to mind. We may not immediately think of digital files as requiring long-term care or management, or as objects that have history, provenance, and authenticity. However, digital files are inherently fragile. If the file format or the technology to view the file is obsolete, meaning it is no longer produced and/or the cost of maintenance or access is prohibitive, then we no longer have all the elements required to display the artwork. Furthermore, if the files of a digital artwork become corrupt or are accidentally deleted and the artist, the artist’s studio, or artist’s estate can not replace the files, the artwork is essentially lost. At the Art Institute of Chicago, visitors may encounter video art projected on walls or screens, such as [Rosalind Nashashibi’s *Vivian’s Garden*](/artworks/246445/vivian-s-garden), or displayed on monitors where the monitors are a sculptural element of the artwork like in [Nam June Paik’s *Family of Robot: Baby*](/artworks/117190/family-of-robot-baby). They may also experience an artwork that not only relies on digital technology but for which born-digital files or software is the artistic medium. In an era of rapid technological change, we are faced with artworks that may not be accessible in the next system upgrade. Artworks such as these are described as *time-based media art*, an umbrella term we use to describe artworks with a durational aspect that visually or audibly unfolds for the audience over a perceived amount of time. In time-based media art conservation, we are focusing our efforts towards understanding how media artworks are created, how the artist intends they should be presented and, equally important, how they will be available for future audiences. ![Still from Charles Ray's Fashions, 1996](https://artic-web.imgix.net/null746595f4-2250-4b59-926d-fa01ba47d4a9/121009_670717.jpg?rect=0%2C0%2C2832%2C2250&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=596&blur=1200&sat=20) Photo featuring a still from Charles Ray’s 6mm-film *Fashions*, 1996, and a 16mm projector **Inevitable Obsolescence** Some digital files are created from analog sources. Super8 and 16mm film were popular home video and artistic mediums in the 20th century, and many artists are still producing works on 16mm. Once it appeared on the consumer market in the 1960s, videotape was an increasingly cost-efficient option for making home videos and for creating moving-image artworks. Unfortunately, film and videotape formats are dependent on equipment and subject to obsolescence and deterioration. They rely on specific technologies in order to be viewed or experienced, many of which are no longer manufactured. ![Still from video Xmas1987](https://artic-web.imgix.net/null2844c9fe-e234-462a-8c4a-40c682a8fda5/Cynthia_xmas1987_bandEdit_02.00_03_06_28.Still001.jpg?rect=6%2C0%2C704%2C477&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=508&blur=1200&sat=20) The author (right), captured for posterity on VHS My family has a wonderful videotape on VHS from Christmas 1987 that includes my grandparents waving at their new video camera in their living room and later my cousin and me starting a guitar and piano band for a not-so-great rendition of the ABCs with my mom and aunt. VHS equipment is no longer produced as of 2016, only about 40 years after it was first introduced. Compare about 50 years of analog video to hundreds of years of oil paintings or thousands of years of clay-based pottery. Which is more transient? ![Still photo from Steve McQueen's Exodus, 1992/97](https://artic-web.imgix.net/nullbe94a9ec-091d-47f7-8668-b89473c918fd/185097_1473826.jpg?rect=0%2C0%2C3000%2C2061&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=515&blur=1200&sat=20) Still from Steve McQueen’s *Exodus* (1992/97), transferred from Super8 to digital video **Art Migration** When the technology becomes obsolete, we preserve the content by migrating or reformatting it, which means moving it from one technology format to another. For example, your home videotape may have been transferred to a DVD, and you may now have a copy of that DVD on your computer desktop. (Ideally, you would create a digital file from the videotape, not the DVD.) Each step is a *migration*. With video art, what we often try to preserve throughout this process—though this is different for every artwork—is a visual quality that is inherent to the image from when it was first created. There is an aesthetic difference between a video made in the 1990s compared to a compressed DVD file or the high-resolution digital videos of today. On older videos, edges and sharp lines may seem blurry or jagged, especially after watching TV on a 2K ultra high-definition flat screen. The migration history of an artwork provides information about what we should expect to see or experience when the work is installed. ![Photo of installed work by Diana Thater. Moluccan Cockatoo Molly Numbers 1-10, 1995](https://artic-web.imgix.net/null5e497386-9b11-4a22-b06d-60fcbf7e3274/IM042212_008-int.jpg?rect=0%2C0%2C3000%2C2000&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=500&blur=1200&sat=20) Moluccan Cockatoo Molly Numbers 1–10, 1995 Diana Thater Tracing the technical history of an artwork is one of the most intriguing aspects of my job. What technology did the artist use to create their work, and was it a conscious choice? As with other forms of art, the tools an artist used can tell us about the time period in which it was created; in time-based media, the tools are cameras and software programs instead of clay molds or paint pallets. As someone who loves digging into a new application to figure out its capabilities, understanding the creative and novel ways artists have used technology to tell a story, present a point of view, or explore a theme is an exciting form of discovery. —Kristin MacDonough, time-based media conservator Interested in exploring digital preservation as a process? The Art Institute of Chicago is hosting the symposium [*UNFOLDING—Production and Process in Time-Based Media Art*](/events/4705/panel-discussions-unfoldingproduction-and-process-in-time-based-media-art)on October 31 and November 1, 2019. The event will bring together artists, curators, conservators, technicians, registrars, collection managers, and related professionals to explore how decisions made during the creation and installation of an artwork can influence what is preserved over time. #### Topics ## Share ## Further Reading ![Two Fayum mummy images side by side](https://artic-web.imgix.net/nulld3191fd5-a291-417f-943e-66bc578d397f/doublecroppedimager.jpg?rect=0%2C0%2C1798%2C1012&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=422&blur=1200&sat=20) ![Shows X-ray images of a wooden bodhisattva that's in the process of conservation](https://artic-web.imgix.net/9b67a3a3-70ef-44fa-80dd-a5fcd48caeff/IM001576_067-web.jpg?rect=200%2C0%2C1017%2C572&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=422&blur=1200&sat=20) ![Infrared phtography of Rembrandt's Old Man with Gold Chain that show a hidden shadow on the wall behind the figure.](https://artic-web.imgix.net/7d2d1f3b-0bd9-46f5-ac56-860ea380c53c/Image5_IR.jpg?rect=0%2C686%2C2452%2C1379&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=422&blur=1200&sat=20) ![Image of poisonous fountain in Dead Time](https://artic-web.imgix.net/989a172c-3b93-4889-9a9d-4511f102ed51/image3.jpeg?rect=0%2C348%2C1629%2C517&auto=format&q=1&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Ccenter&w=750&h=422&blur=1200&sat=20) ## enriched_points.point_title: Rapid Evolution of Software and Hardware
enriched_points.expanded_content: The continuous advancement in software and hardware creates challenges in accessing and preserving digital art, as older tools and platforms become unsupported and outdated.
As technology progresses, compatibility problems arise with legacy formats, making it difficult to retrieve and view artworks created with obsolete software or hardware.
Technological obsolescence risks permanent loss of access to digital artworks, as unsupported formats and failing hardware render significant pieces of cultural heritage inaccessible.
To ensure longevity, digital art requires continuous updates and migrations, necessitating resources and expertise to keep pace with evolving technologies and formats.
